title: Having Crossed Many Hardships, Come, Let Us Carry On This Continuing Work!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; the printed thirteenth-election reference and organisational counts have been verified from the scans; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Our movement's fourteenth general organisational election has proceeded stage by stage, from branch secretaries to district secretaries, both democratically and with a spirit of competition to serve. The dates for electing district secretaries have now been announced. Ours is not a party that merely issues appointments declaring that a certain person alone shall be secretary of a certain district. Since its founding, the DMK has completed thirteen general elections. The thirteenth was held on 27 December 2008, and the fourteenth is now under way. The DMK is not presently the ruling party. Yet do you know the extent of the competition among our members to serve the movement?

The DMK has 8,436,744 members in this fourteenth organisational election. As I have written before, several organisational changes were made between the thirteenth and fourteenth elections. At the thirteenth, there were thirty-six party districts, including Madurai and Coimbatore City. For administrative convenience, we have now divided them into sixty-five districts. Likewise, the former 392 union organisations have become 578.

There are also 129 town organisations, ninety-seven area organisations and 538 town-panchayat organisations. In addition, the movement has 112,247 ward branches: 820 corporation wards, 8,397 town-panchayat wards, 3,735 municipal wards, and 99,295 village-panchayat wards across 12,514 panchayats. Elections have been completed for panchayat wards, municipal wards, town-panchayat wards, unions and towns. Corporation-ward elections are continuing.

Across all India, I know of no party other than the DMK that systematically issues membership cards and, on that basis, conducts elections from the lowest level to the highest. In elections conducted democratically and scientifically in this manner, isolated problems may arise in one or two places. I have learned of a few such minor issues during this fourteenth election. Some complaints may have arisen from resentment against district secretaries or headquarters commissioners. Others may have been made because the complainant's team lost.

Competition is natural in a democratic movement. In a great party such as the DMK, it is understandable that several people will seek the same responsibility. But that competition must be fair, healthy and democratic. It must not become improper, irresponsible or a source of smouldering resentment.
I have already recalled Perarignar Anna's moving words at a meeting on Chennai's Marina on 26 September 1960:
“The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is not merely a political party; it is a movement for social reform.

“For all who are involved in it, the question is not who occupies which place, nor is it important who assumes which responsibility at what time. Each person taking up a responsibility is like taking a position in this movement's battle camp. If enemies try to enter, they will be trapped and crushed between the closeness that binds us.

“The DMK is an organisation created so that each person may work from a particular place. If our opponents imagine that other differences of rank or resentments exist among us, we can only say that they do not know the history of how we grew. We are people who can eat from the same leaf. The only reason we were not born from one mother's womb is that no single mother's womb could bear so many children!”
Every movement comrade who contests, wins, loses the opportunity to win, or casts a vote must never forget these words of Perarignar Anna.

Now that district-secretary elections have been announced and arrangements are under way, contestants must remember something. After our great defeat in the last parliamentary election, some newspapers wrote that one cause was the conduct of a few district secretaries, particularly some who had also held ministerial office. They went so far as to write that district secretaries had behaved like “petty kings.”

In another party, everyone accused might be removed immediately without any inquiry and an announcement issued. But does not the DMK cherish duty, dignity and discipline? Has it not displaced authoritarianism and protected democracy? The General Secretary, our esteemed Professor, Treasurer younger brother M.K. Stalin and I therefore discussed repeatedly how to conduct this election and how the DMK should be led in future.

Day and night, my thoughts are about how to build and protect the movement, how to develop it and what will strengthen it. To refine its functioning further, we must dispel the mistaken impression among a few that the movement has been entrusted to only certain people. We thought that termites must not be allowed to eat into the pillars, the district secretaries, that hold up the DMK's structure. We discussed many times what could be done.

Of the present sixty-five districts, thirty-one are new, and new people will be elected as their secretaries. We considered what should be done in the other thirty-four. After nominations closed, we identified eighteen candidates who were former Ministers or had repeatedly served as district secretaries. The General Secretary, Treasurer Stalin and I summoned them and spoke to them directly.
In a democratic district-secretary election, no one can be forbidden from contesting. We therefore arranged for those wishing to contest to provide an undertaking. It says:

“As resolved, this undertaking is obtained from candidates filing nominations for district secretary so that they will accept and act under party discipline. Those signing and submitting it to the President hereby affirm that they will not demand that they must contest the next Assembly or parliamentary election, nor request a position appointed by the President for themselves or members of their families.”

Only after signing this undertaking are the eighteen contesting the district elections. In some districts, others have filed nominations against them; in a few, no one else is contesting. The movement did not decide that former Ministers or district secretaries must be returned. The undertaking was obtained so that, even if they contest and win, they will not again expect to become MLAs or Ministers.

Elections for five districts, Thanjavur North, Thanjavur South, Tiruchi North, Cuddalore West and Kanniyakumari East, will be held at Anna Arivalayam at 10 a.m. on 19 December 2014. At 3 p.m. that day, Anna Arivalayam will host elections for Nagapattinam North, Nagapattinam South, Coimbatore North, Coimbatore South and Kanniyakumari West. At the same time, elections for Pudukkottai North, Pudukkottai South and Madurai South will be held at Rayapuram Arivagam.

At 10 a.m. on 21 December, elections for Tiruvarur, Karur, Theni and Dharmapuri will be held at Anna Arivalayam. At 3 p.m. there, elections will follow for Namakkal East, Namakkal West, Coimbatore City North and Coimbatore City South.

At 10 a.m. on 22 December, Anna Arivalayam will host elections for Tiruvannamalai North, Villupuram North, Villupuram South, Tiruppur North and Tiruppur South. At 3 p.m. that day, elections will be held there for Ramanathapuram, Tirunelveli East, Tirunelveli West and Tirunelveli Central.

I want everyone to conduct themselves with the understanding that these elections are being held to decide under whose leadership movement work in those districts will be carried out for the next five years. No undemocratic act should be permitted.

Those who win must not imagine that they have defeated some enemy. Those who miss victory are also Udanpirappus of the movement. Tomorrow, we must join with them to face our common opponent in Assembly and parliamentary elections. Winners and those who lost should shake hands and come to meet us. No one should grow arrogant and say, “You dared contest against me? I will make sure no one knows where you are!” They must walk the path of love, righteousness and peace taught by Anna.

Udanpirappus who lose must continue working with those who win, earn a place in the hearts of movement comrades through their labour, and strive to win the next election. It is not good to think, “You defeated me? I will show you!”

When college students divide into two or three teams for a tug-of-war, victory and defeat do not wound their hearts. We have seen that only the spirit of sport comes through. It must be the same with us. We divide into groups for our movement's democratic organisational election, but that division must contain no resentment or hostility, only a spirit of competition and the single shared purpose of protecting and developing the movement's principles. My unquenchable desire is that this spirit should shine through in our internal elections.

Just as we fulfilled Anna's purpose when he was among us, Anna remains among us today. He is alive. Not for the smallest instant has he left us; he is mingled with the feelings of our hearts. We never forget him and never will.
Whatever victories or defeats members experience in this election, let the five great slogans we accepted from the movement's earliest days ring forever in our hearts:
“We shall labour tirelessly in Anna's path;
We shall create a society free from domination;
We shall forever oppose the imposition of Hindi;
We shall renounce violence and conquer poverty;
Federalism at the Centre, autonomy in the States.”

Let us follow Anna's path wholeheartedly in dutiful awareness and dignified conduct. Above all, let us protect discipline from being weakened. Let us never forget the path of Thanthai Periyar, who shaped us for this great task. Let us protect our movement and clear a path for future Dravidian generations to live with self-respect and distinction.
May this fourteenth general election of the movement help the work we have undertaken democratically to flourish ever more strongly and continue without faltering!
With affection,
M.K.
17 December 2014
